title: A Stochastic Approximation Approach for Efficient Decentralized Optimization on Random Networks

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a Fully Stochastic Primal-Dual Algorithm (FSPDA) framework for decentralized stochastic optimization over random, time-varying networks with sparsified communication. The key idea is to encode the randomness of network topology (and optionally coordinate sparsification) into a stochastic augmented Lagrangian whose expected saddle points coincide with KKT solutions of the original consensus optimization problem. Two concrete algorithms are developed: FSPDA-SA (a stochastic gradient descent-ascent method) achieving O(σ/√(nT)) stationarity for smooth nonconvex objectives, and FSPDA-STORM (a momentum-based variance-reduced variant) achieving O(σ^{2/3}/T^{2/3}). Empirical results on MNIST and ImageNet indicate improved communication efficiency over strong baselines under stringent random link activation.
strengths: The paper makes a clear conceptual step by embedding time-varying topology randomness directly into a stochastic augmented Lagrangian, yielding a “fully stochastic” primal-dual view. This sidesteps some difficulties of analyzing products of varying mixing matrices that hamper gradient tracking analyses.

The development of a variance-reduced primal-dual scheme (FSPDA-STORM) for decentralized, time-varying networks appears novel and technically nontrivial, leveraging mean-square smoothness to obtain O(T^{-2/3}) rates.
The connection to EXTRA/GT via a second-order primal-only recursion (Eq. (11)) is insightful and helps position the method relative to established algorithms.
Experiments include both a moderately sized (MNIST, MLP) and a large-scale (ImageNet, ResNet-50) setting, with careful normalization of communication budgets across methods.
Ablations (as referenced in the appendix) over sparsity, data heterogeneity, topology, and momentum provide additional evidence about robustness and practical behavior.
The paper is generally well-structured, with assumptions and step-size conditions stated and a clear separation between SA and STORM variants.
The insight section (fixed-point interpretation of the dual and its relation to tracking average gradients) is helpful in building intuition for the Lyapunov analysis.

Addressing decentralized nonconvex stochastic optimization over random time-varying networks without bounded heterogeneity is of high importance, with practical relevance to unreliable or bandwidth-constrained networks.
The achieved rates align with best-known centralized SGD (linear speedup for SA) and near-optimal nonconvex stochastic lower bounds (for STORM), while accommodating sparse randomized communication.
weaknesses: The gradient used in the primal update deviates from the exact stochastic gradient of the proposed stochastic augmented Lagrangian by replacing A(ξ)ᵀλ with Aᵀλ “to facilitate algorithm development” (Eq. (5) discussion). This introduces a bias term that is not explicitly modeled in Assumption 3.4 (which only controls A(ξ)ᵀA(ξ)x vs AᵀRAx). It is not fully clear how the analysis accounts for this discrepancy rigorously.

FSPDA-STORM requires nontrivial initializations (e.g., local duals and momentum initialized with the global gradient or averages) to meet the stated rate; this is unrealistic in decentralized settings without extra synchronization. While the authors say zero initialization works in practice, the theoretical reliance on special initialization weakens the formal claim.

Assumption 3.8 (mean-square smoothness) is stronger than standard L-smoothness and may limit applicability; the paper should better discuss when it holds for common deep learning losses with data sampling noise.
No error bars or multiple runs; the authors acknowledge this. This limits confidence in the comparative gains, especially for nonconvex deep models.

The ImageNet experiment compares against only two baselines due to resource limits and does not include recent gradient-tracking-based methods with local updates (even if those require synchronization), which makes it harder to isolate the benefit of the proposed framework beyond compression and topology modeling.

The fairness of the bit-budget normalization is critical but under-specified in the main text; for instance, precisely how one-edge random activation with k-coordinate sparsification is matched to local-update periodic communication in terms of wall-clock time, latency, and amortized synchronization overhead should be detailed.

Several typographic artifacts (e.g., “O(σ^2/3/T^2/3)”) and notational inconsistencies can impede readability. The presentation of constants (e.g., in Theorems 3.5 and 3.9) is dense, and the step-size regimes (with many interdependent constants) are difficult to parse without more intuition or simplified corollaries.

The role of σ_A and its scaling with edge/coordinate sampling is only briefly discussed; readers would benefit from explicit examples quantifying σ_A for standard random edge activation probabilities and sparsity levels.

While the paper cites Bianchi et al. (2021) on fully stochastic primal-dual algorithms, the direct methodological and analytical differences are not deeply discussed. Given the reliance on a “fully stochastic” view, a more explicit comparison (assumptions, bias handling, rates, and whether time-varying topologies and sparsification are covered) is warranted.

There are other primal-dual approaches for decentralized optimization (e.g., Prox-PDA and subsequent variants) whose treatment of time-varying graphs and nonconvexity could be more thoroughly contrasted, especially around the augmented Lagrangian design and the dual update structure.
reviews.questions:
1. Can you rigorously justify the substitution of A(ξ)ᵀλ by Aᵀλ in Eq. (5)? Is the resulting gradient estimator unbiased for the stochastic augmented Lagrangian, or do you control the bias via additional assumptions (e.g., on λ’s moments or on E[||A(ξ) − A||²])? Please pinpoint where this is used in the proofs.
2. Could you provide explicit expressions or bounds for σ_A under common activation models (e.g., each edge independently selected with probability p and each coordinate with probability q)? How do these enter the step-size choices?
3. The STORM variant requires initializations that depend on the global gradient and consensus (Eq. (23) discussion). Is there a decentralized warm-start procedure (e.g., a few communication rounds) that achieves the required bounds? How sensitive is the rate to zero initialization in theory?
4. For the ImageNet experiment, can you expand in the main text on the bit accounting, including encoding of coordinates/indices, headers, and whether you account for bidirectional traffic? Also, how were hyperparameters tuned across baselines to ensure fairness?
5. How do the proposed methods behave under partial asynchrony or stragglers in practice? Can you report any ablation or simulation with random compute/communication delays?
6. Can you elaborate on how your rates compare to the best known in time-varying decentralized optimization for convex and strongly convex cases (e.g., Kovalev et al., Li & Lin), and whether your framework could be adapted to recover those optimal rates?
reviews.assessment:
This paper tackles an important and challenging problem: nonconvex decentralized stochastic optimization over random, time-varying topologies with sparse communication. The fully stochastic augmented Lagrangian perspective is elegant and practically meaningful, and the theoretical rates are strong: linear speedup O(σ/√(nT)) for the SA variant and near-optimal O(σ^{2/3}/T^{2/3}) for the STORM variant under mean-square smoothness. The analysis contains interesting ideas (e.g., the v^t tracking term and K-weighted Lyapunov function), and the experiments, while lacking error bars, demonstrate promising communication efficiency on both small and large-scale tasks.

The main reservations are technical and experimental. The modification of the primal gradient (replacing A(ξ)ᵀλ by Aᵀλ) needs a sharper, explicit justification to ensure the claimed convergence bounds fully align with the actual algorithm. The STORM initialization requirements are impractical in decentralized settings and should either be removed in theory or achieved through an explicit, low-cost warm start. Empirically, broader baselines and statistical significance would strengthen the case.

Overall, I view the contribution as significant and promising, with a few important clarifications needed to consolidate the theoretical soundness and practical relevance. With these addressed, the paper would be a valuable addition to the NeurIPS literature on decentralized optimization.
